2.96 StarsBuenos Aires’ Top Five Consulted Public Hospitals in 2025 Earn Lower Reviews With Text

Delays, inappropriate behavior and inefficient medical care are the most common complaints in Google reviews. Despite concerns about their reliability, the platform says on its website that posting “misinformation” is prohibited and users can report fake content.

By Ignacio Grimaldi

An article written for the Lede Program at Columbia University

The most consulted are getting worse reviews. That is the picture painted by official statistics and Google reviews of the five busiest public hospitals in the City of Buenos Aires in 2025. In 2023, when the city government reduced public health spending, a years-long trend of predominantly positive ratings reversed, with negative ratings outnumbering positive ones for the first time. Long wait times, mistreatment and ineffective medical care dominate complaints.

While user reviews may not always reflect genuine experiences and can be influenced by coordinated manipulation or artificially positive ratings, Google says “fake engagement is not allowed and will be removed” and allows users to report policy violations. Even so, this comments are among the first pieces of information people see when searching for a hospital online, potentially shaping a critical decision: whether to seek care at a particular facility or avoid it.

A search for Hospital Penna—the public hospital that handled the largest number of patient visits in Buenos Aires in 2025—illustrates the contrast. Google displays a photo of the hospital, its location and three featured reviews: one five-star rating and two one-star ratings. “Excellent service. Thank you very much. I LOVE HOSPITAL PENNA.” The other two paint a starkly different picture: “Honestly, it’s disgraceful. I was treated terribly.” and “I’ve been waiting at admissions for an hour. Nobody is there, nobody helps you, nobody even tells you what’s going on.”

Representatives of the Ministry of Health did not respond to questions about the Google reviews’ findings.

In 2025, an average of 14,101 patients per day sought care at Buenos Aires’ 31 public hospitals, according to official city statistics. Nearly 30% of those visits were concentrated in just five facilities: Penna, Álvarez, Argerich, Durand and Rivadavia.

Since 2012, the five hospitals have received 4,355 Google reviews which contain text. Among them, ratings range from one star, the lowest possible score, to five stars, the highest. Those two extremes account for 77.9% of all reviews.

Overall, positive reviews still outnumber negative ones. But an analysis by year shows that this pattern changed in 2023. For the first time, one-star reviews exceeded five-star reviews, and that imbalance has continued ever since.

Public health spending also points to 2023 as a turning point. According to official city spending records, accrued health expenditures fell from 241.1 billion pesos to 61.9 billion pesos.

Hospital Penna posted the lowest average rating in 2025, with 2.45 stars. It also treated the largest number of patients that year. Hospital Argerich followed with 2.76 stars, and Hospital Álvarez with 2.99 stars. All three recorded lower ratings than they had in 2023, the year the trend reversed.

The other two hospitals among the city’s five busiest followed a different trajectory. Both improved their reputations relative to 2023. In 2025, Hospital Rivadavia averaged 3.21 stars, while Hospital Durand received 3.22 stars.

Between 2023 and 2025—the period during which negative reviews outnumbered positive ones—the five hospitals received 748 one-star reviews. Complaints about long wait times were the most common. They appeared in 55.3% of one-star reviews, according to an AI-assisted analysis that classified user comments into predefined categories.

One reviewer, Fulvio Torres, wrote about Hospital Penna on May 10, 2025: “We arrived at 3:20 a.m. and weren’t seen until 9:00 a.m. The doctors’ care was awful. In short, if you’re dying, they could practically hold your wake at the hospital before they treat you.”

Many reviews mentioned more than one problem in the same post. A single review could describe excessive waiting times while also alleging mistreatment, inadequate medical care, poor infrastructure or an incorrect diagnosis.

As a result, 55.3% of one-star reviews mentioned delays, 50.8% referred to mistreatment, 17.2% criticized ineffective medical treatment, 16.3% cited poor infrastructure, 4.4% alleged incorrect diagnoses, and 16.4% described other issues that did not fit into those categories.

Delays

Hospital Penna recorded the highest share of reviews complaining about long wait times. Durand, Argerich and Rivadavia followed closely behind, with each exceeding 55%.

Inappropriate behavior

This category includes reviews alleging disrespectful behavior, verbal abuse or other forms of mistreatment by hospital staff. Hospital Álvarez recorded the highest proportion of complaints in this category: 61.9% of its negative reviews referred to alleged mistreatment. One reviewer, identified as “angelo,” wrote on May 24, 2025: “The emergency room administration was terrible. I’d been having symptoms for several days and they were getting worse. The woman at the desk told me, ‘Today we have a lot of emergencies. If you’ve already had those symptoms for a couple of days, you can wait a few more and come back another day.’ You can’t tell someone that without knowing what’s wrong with them. I had to insist before they finally saw me.”

Ineffective medical treatment

Hospital Durand recorded the highest proportion of reviews alleging ineffective medical care. One reviewer, Yamila Ayelén Coria, wrote on March 6, 2025: “Terrible. I went in with severe pelvic pain. They ordered a urine test. I waited three hours and they never came back with the results. I had to go home still in pain and without any answers. Everything was awful.”

Bad infrastructure

Hospital Rivadavia recorded the highest share of infrastructure-related complaints, representing 21.8% of its negative reviews. A reviewer identified as Adrisol Palavecino wrote: “It’s unbelievable. It’s 12:49 a.m. We arrived at 7:30 p.m., and suddenly they told all the patients who had been waiting more than three hours to go outside in the freezing cold because they had to mop the floors. At 12:49 in the morning. It’s outrageous and shameful.”

Incorrect diagnosis

Incorrect diagnosis was the least common complaint among one-star reviews. Hospital Rivadavia recorded the highest proportion in this category, though it represented only 6.9% of its negative reviews. One reviewer, Hanna Gomez, wrote: “I’m alive by pure luck. They sent me home because they said it was just gas. When I went to Hospital Penna, I had emergency surgery because it turned out to be something much more serious.” The reviewer did not specify what the correct diagnosis ultimately was.

After identifying these reviews, which were published more than eleven months ago and still are available online, representatives of the Ministry of Health were asked to comment, but they did not respond.

Negative reviews have continued to outnumber positive ones in 2026. As of July 1, critical reviews remained more common than favorable ones. At the beginning of the year, the Buenos Aires city budget allocated 15% of public spending to public safety, described in an official statement as the government’s “central priority.” Health received a slightly larger share—16.5% of total spending. Whether that investment will change the tendency about users’ reviews should become clearer over the coming months.

Methodology

This story is based on an analysis of Google reviews. The analysis began by identifying the five most consulted public hospitals in Buenos Aires using official records from IDECBA. It then collected 4,355 reviews posted between 2012 and 2026. One-star reviews published between 2023 and 2025 were categorized using artificial intelligence. A human review of the AI classifications found an accuracy rate of 91.3%.
